nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or still clear streams and pools with dense aquatic and marginal vegetation; often among leaf litter and submerged plant roots in the Brahmaputra river basin drainage

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java moss, Anubias, Cryptocoryne, floating plants)
driftwood and rocks providing line-of-sight breaks
low flow — still or gently filtered water
shaded areas and hiding spots for females

diet.notes: Obligate micro-predator. In the wild feeds on small invertebrates, zooplankton, and insect larvae. In captivity strongly prefers live and frozen micro-foods: live microworms, vinegar eels, Grindal worms, baby brine shrimp (BBS/Artemia nauplii), Daphnia, and Moina are staples. Dried and prepared foods are typically refused or eaten reluctantly, especially when newly introduced; some individuals may accept frozen cyclops or bloodworm over time. Feeding exclusively on prepared foods is generally not viable — a steady supply of live or freshly-frozen micro-foods is required for long-term health and coloration.

goodWith: small peaceful nano fish kept in upper water column (ember tetras, chili rasboras)
small non-competing bottom dwellers in spacious tanks
nano shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina) — with caution; adults generally safe but fry may be taken
avoidWith: other Dario dario males unless tank is large with heavy planting and many line-of-sight breaks
similarly-sized or smaller bottom-dwelling fish (microdevario, other badids, small gobies)
fast or boisterous fish that outcompete at feeding
fish small enough to be harassed by territorial males
any fish with similar body shape or coloration (triggers aggression)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Dario dario; male in breeding coloration showing scarlet-and-blue banding.
Wikimedia Commons image of male Dario dario; good color representation.

summary: One of the smallest known fish in the hobby at under 2 cm, the Scarlet Badis is a micro-predator native to Indian Bhutan drainages. Males are brilliantly colored in scarlet and iridescent blue; females are drab and transparent. Intermediate care due to strict live-food requirements and inter-male aggression.
careNotes: The Scarlet Badis (Dario dario) is one of the smallest fish kept in the aquarium hobby, with adult males reaching only about 1.5–2 cm. Despite their diminutive size, they are robust micro-predators with strong territorial instincts. Males display brilliant scarlet flanks with iridescent blue vertical banding during courtship and aggression; females are transparent-beige with faint markings and considerably smaller (around 1–1.5 cm). Badidae was historically treated as a subfamily of Cichlidae, and the species retains many cichlid-like behavioral traits — males establish and defend territories vigorously, displaying to one another with spread fins and engaging in threat behavior or outright combat in confined spaces. A minimum 20 L tank is workable for a single pair, but more space and heavy planting is strongly advisable if keeping multiple males; each male requires its own clear territory with visual barriers. The water should be soft and slightly acidic to neutral (pH 6.5–7.5, gH 3–10), cool to moderate temperature (15–25°C), and very low flow — this species comes from still backwaters and is poorly adapted to current. Leaf litter (Indian almond leaves), fine substrate, Anubias, Java moss, and Cryptocorynes closely replicate the natural biotope. Diet is the most significant husbandry challenge: Scarlet Badis are obligate micro-predators that typically refuse dried or pelleted foods. A reliable supply of live microworms, vinegar eels, Daphnia, Moina, or baby brine shrimp is essential. Without live or high-quality frozen micro-foods, specimens decline and starve. For this reason the species is rated intermediate rather than beginner despite its nano tank footprint. Males may also be kept in a species-only nano setup to simplify feeding and compatibility management.
breedingNotes: Breeding Dario dario in captivity is feasible but requires proper conditioning. Males court females with intense lateral fin-spread displays. Eggs are scattered among fine-leaved plants or Java moss; no parental care is provided and adults will eat eggs and fry if not removed. A moss-heavy breeding tank of 10–20 L, conditioning with live foods, and slightly raised temperature (22–24°C) typically induces spawning. Eggs hatch in approximately 3 days at 24°C; fry are free-swimming after another 2–3 days and require infusoria or micro-nematodes (vinegar eels, microworms cut very small) for the first week before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Fry survival is improved by a species-only setup. Sexing is easy once fish are sub-adult: males develop red-and-blue banding while females remain plain.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Dario dario
primary.notes: Primary source. Comprehensive species profile covering taxonomy, native range (Brahmaputra drainage, India/Bhutan), water parameters (15–25°C, pH 6.0–7.5, hardness 18–90 ppm), adult size (males to 2 cm, females to 1.5 cm), diet (live micro-foods essential), behavior, tank requirements, and breeding notes.

additional.name: FishBase — Dario dario
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy (Badidae, Perciformes), native range (West Bengal India, Brahmaputra basin), adult size (max ~2 cm TL), temperature range, and diet classification (carnivore). Confirmed family Badidae is separate from Cichlidae.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Scarlet Badis Care Guide
additional.notes: Hobbyist trade source confirming tank minimum (10 gallons recommended, 5 gallon possible for single specimen), live food dependence (microworms, baby brine shrimp, Daphnia), territorial male behavior, compatibility guidance, and nano-tank specialist status.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Dario dario profile
additional.notes: UK hobbyist publication. Confirmed feeding difficulty (live micro-foods strongly preferred over frozen/dried), inter-male aggression requiring visual breaks, Badidae family history as former cichlid subfamily, and native biotope description (shallow vegetated pools and streams).

additional.name: TFH Magazine — Dario dario species spotlight
additional.notes: Specialist aquarium publication. Corroborated sexual dimorphism (brilliant male coloration vs drab females), careLevel intermediate due to feeding requirements, water chemistry preferences (soft, slightly acidic), and species suitability as nano-tank specialist for experienced aquarists.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer among fine plants or Java moss; no parental care. Fry require infusoria then micro-nematodes then BBS nauplii. Remove adults after spawning. Conditioning with live foods and a 10–20 L moss-heavy tank required.
